message.content:
Adversaries may modify the operating system of network devices to introduce new capabilities or weaken existing defenses. This can be done by altering the system image file either in storage or in memory during runtime. In storage, adversaries typically use standard procedures like TFTP, FTP, SCP, or console connections to download and overwrite or add new files. In memory, they may use native debug commands or manipulate the boot loader to patch the running OS. This requires administrative access and can involve methods like ROMMONkit for persistent changes.

The impact includes weakened encryption, compromised authentication, and breached perimeter defenses. Adversaries may also add capabilities like keylogging, multi-hop proxy, and port knocking, or produce false outputs to mislead defenders. Techniques like SYNful Knock exemplify such malware.

Mitigation strategies include restricting administrative access, monitoring and validating system image integrity, using secure boot mechanisms, and employing network segmentation to limit the spread of compromised devices. Regularly updating and patching devices can also help prevent exploitation.
